examples.explanation: Start at (0,0). Move right to (0,1): valid. From (0,1), move right to (0,2): valid. From (0,2), move down to (1,2): valid. From (1,2), move down to (2,2): destination, count 1 path. Backtrack: from (0,1), move down to (1,1): obstacle, return 0. From (0,0), move down to (1,0): valid. From (1,0), move right to (1,1): obstacle, return 0. From (1,0), move down to (2,0): valid. From (2,0), move right to (2,1): valid. From (2,1), move right to (2,2): destination, count 1 path. Total: 1 + 1 = 2.
Start at (0,0). Move right to (0,1): obstacle, return 0. Move down to (1,0): valid. From (1,0), move right to (1,1): destination, count 1 path. Total: 1.

approaches.name: Brute Force Recursion O(2^(m+n))
approaches.summary: Recursively explore all possible paths from start to end, checking each cell for obstacles. This naive approach tries every possible movement combination but becomes impractical for larger grids.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Recursion for Unique Paths II
ladders.desc: Complete recursive solution counting all possible paths from top-left to bottom-right in a grid with obstacles.
ladders.explanation: This is the complete brute force recursive solution to the Unique Paths II problem. The intuition is to explore every possible path from the starting cell (0,0) to the ending cell (m-1,n-1) in an m×n grid, where some cells contain obstacles that cannot be traversed. We use recursion to simulate moving either right or down at each step, checking if the current cell is valid (within bounds and not an obstacle). The base cases are: reaching the destination (return 1 path found), hitting an obstacle (return 0), or going out of bounds (return 0). The recursive relation is: paths(i,j) = paths(i+1,j) + paths(i,j+1), where i is the row index and j is the column index. This approach tries all 2^(m+n) possible move combinations in the worst case, making it exponential in time. The space complexity is O(m+n) due to the recursion stack depth. This solution directly implements the brute force approach described, serving as a foundation for understanding why more efficient methods (like dynamic programming) are needed. Common mistakes include not checking for obstacles first (which could lead to infinite recursion), forgetting base cases for out-of-bounds, or incorrectly handling the destination cell. The real code pattern uses a helper recursive function that takes the current coordinates and the grid.

examples.trace: Start at (0,0). Move right to (0,1): valid. From (0,1), move right to (0,2): valid. From (0,2), move down to (1,2): valid. From (1,2), move down to (2,2): destination, count 1 path. Backtrack: from (0,1), move down to (1,1): obstacle, return 0. From (0,0), move down to (1,0): valid. From (1,0), move right to (1,1): obstacle, return 0. From (1,0), move down to (2,0): valid. From (2,0), move right to (2,1): valid. From (2,1), move right to (2,2): destination, count 1 path. Total: 1 + 1 = 2.
Start at (0,0). Move right to (0,1): obstacle, return 0. Move down to (1,0): valid. From (1,0), move right to (1,1): destination, count 1 path. Total: 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Recursive Path Counter Function
ladders.desc: The recursive helper function that counts paths from a given cell to the destination.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core recursive function used in the brute force approach. The intuition is to define a function that, given a current cell (i,j), returns the number of unique paths from that cell to the bottom-right corner of the grid, considering obstacles. It works by recursively exploring two possible moves: down (i+1,j) and right (i,j+1). The base cases handle: if the cell is out of bounds (return 0), if it contains an obstacle (return 0), or if it is the destination (return 1). The recursive step sums the results from moving down and right. This function is called initially from (0,0) to solve the full problem. Time complexity is O(2^(m+n)) in the worst case, as it may explore all possible paths; space complexity is O(m+n) for the recursion stack. This feeds into L0 by being the main algorithm called to compute the total paths. Common mistakes include incorrect base case order (e.g., checking destination after obstacle might miss cases), or not using memoization (which is intentional here for brute force). The real code pattern is a function dfs(i, j, grid) that returns an integer.

examples.trace: Call dfs(0,0). Check: in bounds, no obstacle, not destination. Call dfs(1,0): in bounds, no obstacle, not destination. Call dfs(2,0): out of bounds, return 0. Call dfs(1,1): destination, return 1. So dfs(1,0) returns 0+1=1. Call dfs(0,1): in bounds, no obstacle, not destination. Call dfs(1,1): destination, return 1. Call dfs(0,2): out of bounds, return 0. So dfs(0,1) returns 1+0=1. Total for dfs(0,0): 1+1=2.

Call dfs(0,0). Check: valid. Call dfs(1,0): valid. Call dfs(2,0): out of bounds, return 0. Call dfs(1,1): destination, return 1. So dfs(1,0) returns 0+1=1. Call dfs(0,1): obstacle, return 0. Total: 1+0=1.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Recursive Exploration with Two Choices
ladders.desc: The recursive strategy of exploring down and right moves to count paths.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core recursive logic of exploring two possible moves at each cell: moving down (incrementing row index) or moving right (incrementing column index). The intuition is that from any cell (i,j) in the grid, the only valid moves to reach the bottom-right corner are to go down or right, as per problem constraints (no up or left moves allowed). This creates a binary decision tree where each node represents a cell, and edges represent moves. The total number of paths is the sum of paths from the down move and right move. This recursive decomposition reduces the problem to smaller subproblems until base cases are reached. The time complexity arises from the exponential growth of this tree: at each cell, two recursive calls are made, leading to O(2^(m+n)) in the worst case. Space complexity is O(m+n) for the depth of recursion. This feeds into L1 as the mechanism inside the helper function. Common mistakes include trying to move up or left, or not handling the case where both moves are invalid. The real code pattern involves if-conditions to check validity before making recursive calls.

examples.input: Cell (0,0) in a 2x2 grid with no obstacles
examples.trace: From (0,0), explore down to (1,0): from (1,0), explore down to (2,0) invalid, explore right to (1,1) destination → 1 path. Explore right from (0,0) to (0,1): from (0,1), explore down to (1,1) destination → 1 path, explore right to (0,2) invalid. Sum: 1 + 1 = 2.

examples.input: Cell (0,0) in a 1x2 grid with obstacle at (0,1)
examples.trace: From (0,0), explore down to (1,0) invalid (out of bounds), explore right to (0,1) obstacle → 0 paths. Sum: 0 + 0 = 0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Valid Cell Check and Base Cases
ladders.desc: Checking if a cell is within bounds, not an obstacle, and is the destination.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves validating a cell before proceeding with recursion. The intuition is that recursion must stop when it encounters invalid states: going outside the grid boundaries, hitting an obstacle, or reaching the destination. These checks form the base cases of the recursion. Specifically: if i < 0 or i >= m or j < 0 or j >= n, return 0 (out of bounds); if grid[i][j] == 1, return 0 (obstacle); if i == m-1 and j == n-1, return 1 (destination reached). These conditions ensure the algorithm terminates correctly and counts only valid paths. This is a building block for L2, as it defines when to stop recursion and how to handle edge cases. Time and space analysis for this sub-problem is O(1) per check, as it involves simple comparisons. Common mistakes include checking base cases in the wrong order (e.g., checking destination after obstacle might incorrectly return 0 for a destination that is also an obstacle, but in this problem, destination can be an obstacle, so order matters: check out-of-bounds first, then obstacle, then destination). The real code pattern uses if-elif-else statements.

examples.input: Cell (2,2) in a 3x3 grid with no obstacles
examples.trace: Check: i=2, j=2, m=3, n=3. Not out of bounds (2<3). Grid[2][2]=0, not obstacle. i==m-1 (2==2) and j==n-1 (2==2), so return 1.
Check: i=0, j=3, m=3, n=3. j=3 >= n=3, so out of bounds, return 0 immediately.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Grid Traversal and Indexing
ladders.desc: Accessing and checking values in a 2D grid using row and column indices.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves working with a 2D grid (list of lists in Python) to read values at specific positions. The intuition is that the grid represents the problem space, where each cell has a value: 0 for empty (traversable) and 1 for obstacle. To navigate, we use indices i (row) and j (column), starting from 0. For example, grid[i][j] accesses the cell at row i, column j. This operation is basic but critical for checking obstacles and determining moves. It feeds into L3 as the means to implement base cases. Time complexity is O(1) per access; space is O(1) for the indices. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., using indices starting from 1), confusing rows and columns, or not validating indices before access (which could cause index errors). The real code pattern involves simple array indexing, often within bounds checks first.

examples.trace: Grid has 2 rows and 2 columns. Access grid[0][1]: row 0 is [0,1], column 1 is the second element, which is 1.
Grid has 2 rows and 3 columns. Access grid[1][2]: row 1 is [0,0,0], column 2 is the third element, which is 0.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding 2D Grids and Indices
ladders.desc: Basic idea of a grid as a 2D array with rows and columns indexed from 0.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: understanding a 2D grid or matrix. Imagine a chessboard-like structure with rows (horizontal lines) and columns (vertical lines). Each cell is identified by two numbers: the row index and column index, both starting from 0. For example, in a 3x3 grid, the top-left cell is (0,0), the top-right is (0,2), and the bottom-right is (2,2). This grid can be represented in programming as a list of lists, where each inner list is a row. Accessing a cell means going to the row first, then the column within that row. This foundation is crucial for navigating in the Unique Paths problem. It feeds into L4 as the basis for traversal. Time and space are not analyzed here as it's a conceptual understanding. Common mistakes include thinking indices start at 1, or confusing rows and columns. The real code pattern in Python is grid = [[0,1],[0,0]] for a 2x2 grid.

examples.trace: The grid has 2 rows: row 0 is [0,1], row 1 is [0,0]. For cell (0,1), row index 0 gives [0,1], column index 1 gives the second element: 1.
Only one row: [0,0,0]. For cell (0,2), row index 0 gives [0,0,0], column index 2 gives the third element: 0.

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming with 2D Array O(m*n)
approaches.summary: Use a 2D DP array where dp[i][j] stores number of ways to reach cell (i,j). Initialize first row/column with 1s until obstacles, then fill grid using dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1] for obstacle-free cells. This is the standard DP solution for this problem.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Unique Paths II with 2D DP
ladders.desc: Complete solution counting unique paths in a grid with obstacles using 2D dynamic programming.
ladders.explanation: This problem asks us to find the number of unique paths from the top-left corner (0,0) to the bottom-right corner (m-1,n-1) in an m×n grid, where some cells contain obstacles that cannot be passed. The standard DP approach uses a 2D array dp where dp[i][j] stores the number of ways to reach cell (i,j). The intuition is that to reach any cell (except obstacles), you must come from either the cell above (i-1,j) or the cell to the left (i,j-1), so dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. We initialize the first row and column: starting from (0,0), we set dp[0][0] = 1 if it's not an obstacle, then propagate 1s along the first row and column until we hit an obstacle, after which all subsequent cells in that row/column become 0 (unreachable). Then we fill the rest of the grid row by row, applying the recurrence only for obstacle-free cells. The final answer is dp[m-1][n-1]. Time complexity is O(m*n) as we visit each cell once, and space complexity is O(m*n) for the DP table. This connects to L1 by implementing the complete grid traversal and obstacle handling. Common mistakes include not handling the start/end cells being obstacles (should return 0), incorrect initialization of first row/column after obstacles, and forgetting to skip obstacle cells during the main loop. Real code pattern: def uniquePathsWithObstacles(obstacleGrid): m,n = len(obstacleGrid), len(obstacleGrid[0]); dp = [[0]*n for _ in range(m)]; for i in range(m): if obstacleGrid[i][0]==1: break; dp[i][0]=1; for j in range(n): if obstacleGrid[0][j]==1: break; dp[0][j]=1; for i in range(1,m): for j in range(1,n): if obstacleGrid[i][j]==0: dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j]+dp[i][j-1]; return dp[m-1][n-1].

examples.trace: Grid: 2x2 with obstacle at (0,1). Initialize dp: dp[0][0]=1. First row: dp[0][1] is obstacle, so break, leaving dp[0][1]=0. First column: dp[1][0]=1. Fill: (1,1): dp[1][1]=dp[0][1]+dp[1][0]=0+1=1. Result: 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — DP Grid Filling with Obstacle Check
ladders.desc: Implement the core DP recurrence to fill the grid while skipping obstacles.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on the main loop that fills the DP table after initialization. The intuition is that for each cell (i,j) (with i>0 and j>0), if it's not an obstacle, the number of ways to reach it is the sum of ways from the cell above and the cell to the left. This works because paths are only allowed to move down or right, so these are the only two possible previous cells. The algorithm: after initializing the first row and column, iterate i from 1 to m-1 and j from 1 to n-1. For each cell, check if obstacleGrid[i][j] == 0 (no obstacle). If true, set dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]; if false (obstacle), leave dp[i][j] as 0 (default). Time complexity for this sub-routine is O((m-1)*(n-1)) ≈ O(m*n), and space is O(1) extra beyond the DP table. This feeds into L0 by being the central computation after setup. Common mistakes include not checking obstacles in the main loop (leading to incorrect sums over obstacles) or incorrectly indexing outside bounds. Real code pattern: for i in range(1, m): for j in range(1, n): if obstacleGrid[i][j] == 0: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1].

examples.trace: Start with dp as given. i=1,j=1: obstacleGrid[1][1]=1 (obstacle), skip, dp[1][1] stays 0. i=1,j=2: obstacleGrid[1][2]=0, dp[1][2]=dp[0][2]+dp[1][1]=1+0=1. i=2,j=1: obstacleGrid[2][1]=0, dp[2][1]=dp[1][1]+dp[2][0]=0+1=1. i=2,j=2: obstacleGrid[2][2]=0, dp[2][2]=dp[1][2]+dp[2][1]=1+1=2. Resulting dp: [[1,1,1],[1,0,1],[1,1,2]].

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Recurrence for Path Counting
ladders.desc: Understand and apply the recurrence relation dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1] for obstacle-free cells.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the fundamental recurrence relation that drives the DP solution. The intuition is combinatorial: to reach cell (i,j), you must have last moved from either (i-1,j) (down move) or (i,j-1) (right move). Since moves are only down or right, these are the only predecessors, and paths are unique sequences of moves, so the total ways is the sum of ways to reach each predecessor. This recurrence holds only if (i,j) is not an obstacle; if it is, dp[i][j] = 0. The algorithm involves computing this sum iteratively. Time and space are O(1) per cell application. This is the core technique feeding into L1 for grid filling. Common mistakes include applying the recurrence to obstacle cells or missing base cases (first row/column). Real code pattern: if obstacleGrid[i][j] == 0: ways = ways_from_above + ways_from_left.

examples.trace: Cell is not an obstacle. Compute dp[i][j] = 3 (from above) + 2 (from left) = 5.
Cell is an obstacle, so dp[i][j] remains 0 regardless of predecessors.

ladders.title: Building Block — Grid Initialization with Obstacles
ladders.desc: Initialize the first row and column of the DP table, handling obstacles.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept sets up the base cases for the DP recurrence. The intuition is that to move along the first row (row 0), you can only come from the left, and along the first column (column 0), only from above. Starting at (0,0), if it's not an obstacle, dp[0][0] = 1 (one way to start). For the first row: iterate j from 1 to n-1, set dp[0][j] = 1 if no obstacle and all previous cells in row are 1; if an obstacle is encountered at some j, set dp[0][j] = 0 and break (all further cells in row are unreachable). Similarly for first column: iterate i from 1 to m-1, set dp[i][0] = 1 if no obstacle and all previous cells in column are 1; break on obstacle. Time complexity is O(m+n), space O(1) extra. This feeds into L2 by providing the initial values for the recurrence. Common mistakes include not breaking on obstacles or incorrectly setting dp[0][0] when it's an obstacle. Real code pattern: if obstacleGrid[0][0]==0: dp[0][0]=1 else 0; for j in range(1,n): if obstacleGrid[0][j]==0 and dp[0][j-1]==1: dp[0][j]=1 else break; similarly for column.

examples.trace: Start: dp[0][0]=1 (no obstacle). j=1: obstacleGrid[0][1]=0, dp[0][1]=1. j=2: obstacleGrid[0][2]=1 (obstacle), set dp[0][2]=0 and break. j=3: not reached, dp[0][3] remains 0. Result: [1,1,0,0].
Start: dp[0][0]=1. i=1: obstacleGrid[1][0]=0, dp[1][0]=1. i=2: obstacleGrid[2][0]=1, set dp[2][0]=0 and break. i=3: not reached, dp[3][0] remains 0. Result: [1,1,0,0].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Check and Skip Obstacle Cells
ladders.desc: Identify obstacle cells in a grid and handle them by skipping or zeroing.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves detecting obstacles (represented as 1 in the input grid) and ensuring they are not included in path counts. The intuition is that obstacles block movement, so any cell with an obstacle has zero ways to reach it, and it cannot be used as a predecessor for other cells. The algorithm is simple: when processing a cell (i,j), check if obstacleGrid[i][j] == 1. If yes, treat it as inaccessible (e.g., set dp value to 0 or skip computation). This operation is applied during DP table initialization and filling. Time complexity is O(1) per check, space O(1). This feeds into L3 and L2 by enabling correct DP updates. Common mistakes include forgetting to check obstacles or misinterpreting grid values (0 vs 1). Real code pattern: if grid[i][j] == 1: # obstacle, do nothing or set dp to 0.

examples.trace: At row 0, column 1, grid[0][1] = 1, so it is an obstacle. Mark as inaccessible.
At row 1, column 0, grid[1][0] = 1, so it is an obstacle. Skip in computations.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — 2D Array Traversal in Row-Major Order
ladders.desc: Iterate through a 2D grid row by row, accessing each cell.
ladders.explanation: This is the simplest prerequisite concept: navigating a 2D array (grid) systematically. The intuition is that a 2D array can be represented as a list of rows, each row being a list of columns. To visit every cell, we use nested loops: an outer loop for rows (i from 0 to m-1) and an inner loop for columns (j from 0 to n-1). This is called row-major order. It ensures we process each cell exactly once, which is essential for DP where we compute values based on previously visited cells. Time complexity is O(m*n) for full traversal, space O(1) extra. This feeds into L4 by providing the structure for obstacle checks. Common mistakes include incorrect loop bounds (off-by-one errors) or confusing row and column indices. Real code pattern: for i in range(m): for j in range(n): # access grid[i][j].

examples.trace: i=0: j=0 -> cell (0,0) value 1; j=1 -> cell (0,1) value 2. i=1: j=0 -> cell (1,0) value 3; j=1 -> cell (1,1) value 4.

approaches.name: Space-Optimized DP O(m*n)
approaches.summary: Use only a 1D array representing the current row, updating it iteratively as we process each row. This reduces space usage while maintaining the same DP logic - each cell's value depends on its left neighbor (same row) and value from previous row.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Space-Optimized DP Solution for Unique Paths II
ladders.desc: Complete implementation using 1D DP array to count unique paths in a grid with obstacles.
ladders.explanation: This solution solves the Unique Paths II problem using space-optimized dynamic programming. The intuition is that we only need the previous row's values to compute the current row, so we can use a 1D array of size n (columns) instead of a full m×n matrix. Each cell dp[j] represents the number of ways to reach column j in the current row. We initialize dp[0] = 1 if the starting cell is not an obstacle, otherwise 0. For each cell (i,j), if it's an obstacle, we set dp[j] = 0. Otherwise, dp[j] = dp[j] (from above) + dp[j-1] (from left), where dp[j] from above is the value from previous iteration (same column, previous row), and dp[j-1] is from current iteration (same row, previous column). We process rows from top to bottom, and within each row, columns from left to right. Time complexity is O(m×n) as we visit each cell once. Space complexity is O(n) for the 1D array. Connection to L1: This builds upon the row processing subroutine by applying it to all rows. Common mistakes include not handling the first row/column correctly when obstacles are present, and forgetting to reset dp[j] to 0 for obstacle cells. Real code pattern: Initialize dp array with zeros, set dp[0] based on start cell. For each row i: For each column j: If obstacleGrid[i][j] == 1: dp[j] = 0. Else if j > 0: dp[j] += dp[j-1]. Return dp[n-1].

examples.trace: Grid: 3x3 with obstacle at (1,1). Initialize dp = [1,0,0] (start at (0,0)=0). Row 0: j=0: dp[0]=1 (no obstacle). j=1: dp[1] = dp[1] (0) + dp[0] (1) = 1. j=2: dp[2] = dp[2] (0) + dp[1] (1) = 1. dp = [1,1,1]. Row 1: j=0: obstacleGrid[1][0]=0, dp[0]=1 (from above). j=1: obstacleGrid[1][1]=1, dp[1]=0. j=2: obstacleGrid[1][2]=0, dp[2] = dp[2] (1 from above) + dp[1] (0) = 1. dp = [1,0,1]. Row 2: j=0: dp[0]=1 (from above). j=1: dp[1] = dp[1] (0 from above) + dp[0] (1) = 1. j=2: dp[2] = dp[2] (1 from above) + dp[1] (1) = 2. Final dp[2] = 2.

Grid: 2x2 with obstacle at (0,1). Initialize dp = [1,0]. Row 0: j=0: dp[0]=1. j=1: obstacleGrid[0][1]=1, dp[1]=0. dp = [1,0]. Row 1: j=0: dp[0]=1 (from above). j=1: dp[1] = dp[1] (0 from above) + dp[0] (1) = 1. Final dp[1] = 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Process a Single Row with 1D DP Update
ladders.desc: Update the DP array for one row given the previous row's values and obstacle information.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine processes a single row of the grid to update the 1D DP array. The intuition is that for each column j in the current row, the number of ways to reach (i,j) is the sum of ways from above (i-1,j) and from left (i,j-1), unless it's an obstacle. Since we're using a 1D array, dp[j] initially holds the value from above (from previous row). We update it in-place: if cell (i,j) is an obstacle, set dp[j]=0; otherwise, if j>0, add dp[j-1] (from left in current row) to dp[j]. We must process columns left to right so dp[j-1] is updated before dp[j]. Time complexity for one row is O(n). Space is O(1) extra beyond the DP array. Connection to L0: This is repeated for each row to solve the full problem. Common mistakes include processing columns right to left (which would use wrong left values) or not resetting dp[j] for obstacles. Real code pattern: For j from 0 to n-1: If obstacleRow[j] == 1: dp[j] = 0. Else if j > 0: dp[j] += dp[j-1].

examples.trace: Start dp = [1,2,1]. j=0: obstacleRow[0]=0, dp[0]=1 (unchanged). j=1: obstacleRow[1]=1, dp[1]=0. j=2: obstacleRow[2]=0, dp[2] = dp[2] (1) + dp[1] (0) = 1. Final dp = [1,0,1].
Start dp = [1,0,0]. j=0: obstacleRow[0]=0, dp[0]=1. j=1: obstacleRow[1]=0, dp[1] = dp[1] (0) + dp[0] (1) = 1. j=2: obstacleRow[2]=0, dp[2] = dp[2] (0) + dp[1] (1) = 1. Final dp = [1,1,1].

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Transition with Space Optimization
ladders.desc: The recurrence relation and how to implement it using only a 1D array.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the DP recurrence: ways(i,j) = 0 if obstacleGrid[i][j]=1, else ways(i-1,j) + ways(i,j-1), with base case ways(0,0)=1 if no obstacle. The space optimization insight is that when computing row i, we only need row i-1 and the current row's left values. We use a 1D array dp where dp[j] stores ways(i,j) during computation of row i. Initially, dp[j] holds ways(i-1,j) from previous row. As we process left to right, dp[j-1] becomes ways(i,j-1). So we update: if obstacle, dp[j]=0; else dp[j] = dp[j] (from above) + dp[j-1] (from left). This eliminates the need for a 2D matrix. Time for one update is O(1), space is O(n). Connection to L1: This logic is applied per cell in the row processing. Common mistakes include incorrect base case handling or misunderstanding the in-place update order. Real code pattern: For each cell: If obstacle: dp[j] = 0. Else: dp[j] = dp[j] + (dp[j-1] if j>0 else 0).

examples.trace: dp initially has values from above: [1,2,3]. For j=1, dp[1] currently is 2 (from above). Since j>0, add dp[0]=1 (from left). So dp[1] = 2 + 1 = 5. dp becomes [1,5,3].
dp initially: [0,1,0]. For j=2, since it's an obstacle, set dp[2]=0 regardless of previous values. dp becomes [0,1,0].

ladders.title: Building Block — 1D Array Initialization and Iteration
ladders.desc: How to set up and traverse a 1D array for DP, handling the first row specially.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves initializing a 1D DP array for the first row and then iterating through subsequent rows. The intuition is that the first row has no 'above' values, so we initialize based on obstacles: dp[0]=1 if start cell is not an obstacle, else 0. For other columns in first row, if no obstacle, dp[j] = dp[j-1] (only from left), else 0. After initialization, we iterate through rows 1 to m-1, updating dp using the row processing logic. Time for initialization is O(n), space is O(n). Connection to L2: This provides the initial state for the DP recurrence. Common mistakes include incorrect initialization when obstacles block the first row/column, or off-by-one errors in row indices. Real code pattern: Initialize dp with zeros. If obstacleGrid[0][0]==0: dp[0]=1. For j from 1 to n-1: If obstacleGrid[0][j]==0: dp[j]=dp[j-1]. Else: dp[j]=0. Then for i from 1 to m-1: process row i.

examples.trace: Initialize dp=[0,0,0,0]. Start: obstacleGrid[0][0]=0, so dp[0]=1. j=1: obstacleGrid[0][1]=0, dp[1]=dp[0]=1. j=2: obstacleGrid[0][2]=1, dp[2]=0. j=3: obstacleGrid[0][3]=0, but since dp[2]=0, dp[3]=0 (cannot reach past obstacle). Final dp=[1,1,0,0].
Initialize dp=[0,0,0]. Start: obstacleGrid[0][0]=1, so dp[0]=0. j=1: obstacleGrid[0][1]=0, but dp[0]=0, so dp[1]=0. j=2: similarly dp[2]=0. Final dp=[0,0,0].

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Array Element Access and Update
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations of reading from and writing to array indices.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves accessing and updating elements in a 1D array using indices. The intuition is that arrays allow O(1) access to any element via its index. For DP, we need to read dp[j] (current value) and dp[j-1] (left neighbor), then update dp[j] with a new value. Steps: 1. Check if index is within bounds (0 to n-1). 2. Read value at index j: val = dp[j]. 3. If j>0, read value at j-1: left_val = dp[j-1]. 4. Compute new value: new_val = 0 if obstacle, else val + left_val (if j>0) or val (if j=0). 5. Write new value: dp[j] = new_val. Time O(1), space O(1). Connection to L3: This is used repeatedly in array initialization and updates. Common mistakes include index out of bounds (e.g., accessing dp[-1]) or using wrong indices. Real code pattern: If j >= 0 and j < n: current = dp[j]; if j > 0: left = dp[j-1]; else: left = 0; dp[j] = new_value.

examples.trace: Start dp=[1,2,3]. Access dp[1]: value is 2. Update dp[1] to 5. Now dp=[1,5,3].
Start dp=[4,0]. Access dp[0]: value is 4. Update dp[0] to 0. Now dp=[0,0].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Indexing
ladders.desc: Understanding 1D arrays, indices, and basic traversal.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers 1D arrays: a contiguous collection of elements accessible by indices starting at 0. The intuition is that arrays store multiple values of the same type in order. Key concepts: 1. Declaration: e.g., dp = [0]*n creates an array of n zeros. 2. Indexing: dp[j] accesses element at position j (0-based). 3. Length: n = len(dp). 4. Traversal: use a loop for j in range(n) to visit each element. 5. Bounds: indices must be 0 ≤ j < n. For DP, we'll create an array to store intermediate results. Time to access is O(1), space is O(n). Connection to L4: This provides the data structure for DP operations. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., using range(1,n+1)) or misunderstanding zero-based indexing. Real code pattern: n = 5; dp = [0]*n; for j in range(n): dp[j] = some_value.

examples.input: Create array of size 3 with all zeros
examples.trace: n=3. Initialize dp = [0]*3. This creates a list with 3 elements: index 0:0, index 1:0, index 2:0.

examples.input: Traverse array [1,2,3] and print indices and values
examples.trace: Array dp = [1,2,3]. Loop j from 0 to 2: j=0, dp[0]=1; j=1, dp[1]=2; j=2, dp[2]=3.
